content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Audit/Revision • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5640              322-5901            322-6441                322-6644

February 17, 1982


Harlan Van Wye
Legal Counsel
County Office of Education
100 Skyport Drive
San Jose, CA 95115

Dear Harlan,

     Thank you for your telephone call requesting advice
from this office. I apologize for the delay in my sending
you written confirmation of my advice. The facts, as you
presented them to me, are that the Santa Clara County School
Board owns and operates an educational television station.
As a fundraiser, the station would like to advertise to its
viewers a tour of England being offered by a travel company.
In exchange for its promotion of the event, the T.V. station
will receive $100 for each viewer that signs up and two of
the station's employees will be allowed to go on the tour
without charge. These employees will act as chaperons and
may engage in some station business, however, the tour is
primarily meant for their enjoyment.

     You indicated that no decision had been made as to
which two employees will receive the free tours, although
you speculated that a likely candidate would be an employee
who has been involved in arranging for the fundraiser. The
final decisions on whether to have the fundraiser, and on
who will go on the tour, rest with the County Superintendent.

     You have asked how the disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act apply to those employees who ultimately
receive the free trips. In answering this, I will assume
that the employees are designated in the County's Conflict
of Interest Code.

     The travel agency is providing the station with money
($100 for each person signing up) and with two free trips,
in exchange for tour publicity and the efforts of the television

Harlan Van Wye
February 17, 1982
Page Two


station's staff in the promotion. The trips, by their very
nature, are intended to be passed on to the employees, subject
only to the station's determination as to which two employees
will receive them. The employees, therefore, should report
the trips as income received from the travel agency on their
Statements of Economic Interest.

You also asked whether the disqualification provisions of
the Political Reform Act would prevent the employee who has been
primarily responsible for planning the tour promotion from being
one of the two employees chosen to go on the trip.

As you know, the Act prohibits public officials from making
or participating in the making of a governmental decision in
which he knows or has a reason to know he has a financial interest.
(Government Code Section 87100.) You indicated to me that, during
the time this employee has been working on the promotion, no
formal decision was made as to who will go on the trip, nor is
there any type of tacit understanding as to which the recipients
will be. Under these circumstances, the employee cannot know
that she was going to receive the trip and, therefore, it is not
foreseeable that she had a financial interest in decisions involving
the promotion. Therefore, she does not have to disqualify herself.

However, once a formal or informal decision is reached as to
which two employees will receive the free trips, the two employees
must disqualify themselves from making or participating in decisions
which have a foreseeable material financial effect on either the
amount of income the employee will receive (the value of the
trip) or on the travel agency (the source of the income). (See
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copy enclosed.)

If you have any additional questions, please feel free to
call me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

James Shank McCran
Counsel
Legal Division

JM:plh
Enclosure

embedding.qa_text: February 17, 1982 Harlan Van Wye Legal Counsel County Office of Education 100 Skyport Drive San Jose, CA 95115 Dear Harlan, Thank you for your telephone call requesting advice from this office. I apologize for the delay in my sending you written confirmation of my advice. The facts, as you presented them to me, are that the Santa Clara County School Board owns and operates an educational television station. As a fundraiser, the station would like to advertise to its viewers a tour of England being offered by a travel company. In exchange for its promotion of the event, the T.V. station will receive $100 for each viewer that signs up and two of the station's employees will be allowed to go on the tour without charge. These employees will act as chaperons and may engage in some station business, however, the tour is primarily meant for their enjoyment. You indicated that no decision had been made as to which two employees will receive the free tours, although you speculated that a likely candidate would be an employee who has been involved in arranging for the fundraiser. The final decisions on whether to have the fundraiser, and on who will go on the tour, rest with the County Superintendent. You have asked how the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to those employees who ultimately receive the free trips. In answering this, I will assume that the employees are designated in the County's Conflict of Interest Code. The travel agency is providing the station with money ($100 for each person signing up) and with two free trips, in exchange for tour publicity and the efforts of the television station's staff in the promotion. The trips, by their very nature, are intended to be passed on to the employees, subject only to the station's determination as to which two employees will receive them. The employees, therefore, should report the trips as income received from the travel agency on their Statements of Economic Interest. You also asked whether the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act would prevent the employee who has been primarily responsible for planning the tour promotion from being one of the two employees chosen to go on the trip. As you know, the Act prohibits public officials from making or participating in the making of a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 87100.) You indicated to me that, during the time this employee has been working on the promotion, no formal decision was made as to who will go on the trip, nor is there any type of tacit understanding as to who the recipients will be. Under these circumstances, the employee cannot know that she was going to receive the trip and, therefore, it is not foreseeable that she had a financial interest in decisions involving the promotion.
